identificationRemarks:
Identification only and exclusively to the level of the species complex sensu lato
Fruiting bodies small to medium. Endoperidium; light bluish with white-brown discoloration [also with a whitish bloom]; flattened spherical in appearance; set on a distinct pseudostipa; with a very distinct apophysis ending at the bottom in a distinct edge. Peristome conical-pointed; distinctly striated; although this striation is invisible in some specimens. Exoperidium with 7-9 rays; symmetrical or asymmetrical; with thickenings at the tips of the triangular arms. Colors beige; beige-brown; beige-pink; scaly; fibrous; and less than elegant in overall appearance.

identificationRemarks:
Probably a complex of 2-3 ecological and morphological lineages from the Geastrum minimum/pectinatum/striatum/schmidelii group.
Accurate species identification is impossible based on macromorphology.

Visual macromorphology:
Diameter of the found fruiting body less than 5 cm. Exoperidium: 7-pointed star; with sharp; pointed arms; outer layer abundantly covered with soil; with fine mineral particles; very dark through it; almost black; inner pseudoparenchymatous layer yellow; ochre-yellow; turning brown towards the edges of the arms; on some arms with brown stripes; on others with a brown-spotted surface; some of the arms were sharply bent under the fruiting body; as if someone had stepped on the mushroom. Endoperidium: composed of two parts; differing significantly in color. Larger upper part - spherical; moderately flattened ball [but not as strongly as in the G. striatum complex]; dark graphite in color with a distinct navy blue tinge; surface slightly rough; not smooth; it merges smoothly into a distinct; rounded apophysis [without any sharp edges; as in the G. striatum complex]; and this apophysis merges smoothly into a pseudostipa with a height approximately equal to the diameter of its base; pseudocylindrical shape; most resembling an hourglass. Color: light nut-brown with white spots and speckles; between it and the higher; larger part of the endoperidium; a distinct whitish; two-dimensional outline; a circle; is visible; without cristate zigzags. Additionally; where the pseudostipa penetrates the exoperidium; a faintly marked two-dimensional [i.e.; flat] white circle/outline can be seen. Peristome: is the darkest element of the endoperidium; it appears to be poorly demarcated by a whitish outline/circle from the rest of the spore sac surface. Shape: deformed pyramidal [more of an angular solid than a solid of revolution]; with a height approximately equal to the diameter of its base area; striation - if present; is quite weak; different from that of G. pectinatum s. p. Spore print: abundant; dark brown; almost black [more black than brown]

occurrenceRemarks: "Count" includes: 84 fruiting bodies - the first visual morphotype; 25 fruiting bodies - the second visual morphotype
identificationRemarks:
The first visual morphotype: -  larger; more massive fruiting bodies; when viewed from a distance at the endoperidium - no "eye effect"; i.e. a clearly visible "dark pupil" on a light background. 

The second visual morphotype - 
Fruiting bodies smaller; finer; more delicate; the length of the stem is much greater than the diameter of the endoperidium; when looking at the endoperidium from a distance - a very clear "eye effect"; i.e. you can easily see the "dark pupil" against the light background of the "eyeball". A working visual description of the peristome: When viewed from the side; the endoperidium appears as a small cylindrical tube embedded in a sphere [i.e.; the endoperidium]; with part of this tube clearly protruding above the surface of the sphere. Viewing the spore opening directly from above reveals a "pitch-black" interior surrounded by a white; circular ring with smooth edges—though this is a three-dimensional structure; this ring represents the upper edge of the aforementioned "cylindrical tube." The surface of the endoperidium; directly adjacent to the peristome; resembles a metallic or dark gray; blackish-gray; bordering on black; ring.
Description of the stipe: distinctly longer than the diameter of the endoperidium; thin; brittle; rachitic; darkening towards the base to almost black/black
